subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 10.1 defines negative numbers, the integer number line, and the operations of addition and subtraction through the rich, multi-layered context of Bela's Building of Fun — a multi-storied building with floors both above and below ground. The ground floor is Floor 0, floors above are positive, and floors below are negative. A lift with '+' (up) and '–' (down) buttons introduces the notation +n and –n for movements. Floor numbering extends from below-ground shops like the Toy Store (Floor –1), Video Games (Floor –2), and Cinema (Floor –3) to above-ground venues like the Food Court (Floor +1), Art Centre (+2), Books (+3), and Ice Cream shop (+4). Students encounter the rule Starting Floor + Movement = Target Floor for addition, then discover that combining button presses is itself addition, leading naturally to the concept of additive inverse (the 'Back to Zero' idea: pressing +3 and then –3 returns to Floor 0). Comparison of integers is introduced using floor position: Floor –4 is lower and therefore less than Floor –3, establishing the ordering of integers on a vertical line. The section then applies subtraction as 'finding the movement needed': Target Floor – Starting Floor = Movement needed, with three worked examples covering (–1)–(–2), (–1)–(+3), and (+2)–(–2). This is extended to larger numbers through a mine context where levels range from –200 to +180 m, reinforcing addition and subtraction with larger integers. An infinite lift metaphor introduces the full integer number line (…–4,–3,–2,–1,0,1,2,3,4,…), connects the lift to the horizontal number line, and reveals that subtracting a negative number equals adding the corresponding positive. The subsections 'Adding and subtracting larger numbers', 'Adding, subtracting, and comparing any numbers', 'Back to the number line', 'Using the unmarked number line', and 'Converting subtraction to addition and addition to subtraction' all belong to this section's unfolding arc. Students apply these ideas across numerous Figure it Out sets involving both small and large integers.

Section 10.2 applies the token model — a physical/visual representation using positive (green) and negative (red) tokens — to perform addition and subtraction of integers. The section opens with the lift attendant collecting positive and negative tokens in his pocket; after an hour, the number of tokens determines his floor. A zero pair (one positive + one negative = 0) is introduced as the key cancellation tool for addition. Students add integers by laying out tokens, removing all zero pairs, and reading the remainder. Subtraction using tokens is then demonstrated: taking away tokens of the required sign from an existing pool, with the key trick of adding zero pairs when there are not enough tokens of the required type to take away. Three worked examples — (+5)–(+6), (–7)–(–5), and +4–(–6) — demonstrate the technique systematically. The token model provides a concrete, manipulative justification for why subtracting a negative is the same as adding a positive.

Section 10.3 applies integers to three real-world contexts. The first is banking (credits and debits): starting with a balance of ₹100, students track credits as positive changes and debits as negative changes, including the possibility of a negative balance. Figure it Out problems invite students to compute bank balances with multiple credits and debits. The second context is geographical cross-sections, where height above sea level is positive and below sea level is negative, using a cross-section chart with seven labelled points (A through G). Students read heights, order points, and research real-world extremes (Mount Everest, Challenger Deep). The third context is temperature in degrees Celsius, where 0°C is the freezing point of water, temperatures above are positive, and temperatures below are negative. Students identify sub-zero places in India and match temperature readings to times of day for Leh, Ladakh in November. These real-world connections consolidate integer sense and comparison.

Section 10.4 applies integers to two exploratory puzzle activities. The first is a 'hollow integer grid' — a 3×3 grid where the border cells (top row, bottom row, left column, right column) must all sum to the same 'border sum'. Two example grids are given (border sum 0 and border sum –3), and students complete three more grids with specified border sums (+4, –2, –4), exploring multiple solutions and investigating which grids admit more than one filling. The second activity is an 'amazing grid of numbers' — a 4×4 integer grid where students circle any number, strike out its row and column, circle another unstruck number, and repeat; the sum of the four circled numbers is always the same regardless of choices. Two further grids are provided for exploration. A final standalone Figure it Out set consolidates integer arithmetic and ordering: writing integers between pairs, summing to –8, exploring sums achievable with integer dice, solving a table of eight arithmetic expressions, finding historical years using the integer line (including BCE considerations), completing sequences, creating expressions with integer cards, and determining the sign of results of various addition/subtraction combinations. The section closes with an integer Snakes and Ladders game extending from –50 to +50.

Section 10.5 recalls the historical origins of integers, noting that negative numbers and zero were first formalised in Asia. Chinese work (Nine Chapters on Mathematical Art, c. 1st–2nd century CE) used red and black rods analogous to the chapter's tokens. India's Kautilya (Arthaśhāstra, c. 300 BCE) recognised negative bank balances; the Bakśhālī manuscript (c. 300 CE) used a special symbol for negatives. Brahmagupta's Brāhma-sphuṭa-siddhānta (628 CE) gave the first complete, explicit rules for addition and subtraction of positive numbers, negative numbers, and zero — essentially forming the modern integer ring. His five rules for addition and five rules for subtraction are quoted in the section. European resistance to negative numbers persisted into the 18th century (Lazare Carnot), but integers are now foundational and paved the way for algebra.

enumerated_activities.description: Students identify floors and their numbers in Bela's Building of Fun from a diagram, and determine what to press on the lift to travel four floors up and three floors down.

enumerated_worked_examples.book_ref: Example (Addition to keep track of movement), p.245
enumerated_worked_examples.description: Starting from Food Court (Floor +1), pressing +2 in the lift leads to target floor (+1)+(+2)=+3, illustrating the rule Starting Floor + Movement = Target Floor.
Gurmit presses +2 by mistake then –3 to correct; result is (+2)+(–3)=–1, illustrating that combining button presses is addition.
Pressing +3 and then –3 returns to Floor 0; introduces the concept of additive inverse. (+3)+(–3)=0.
Target Floor –1, Starting Floor –2: movement needed is (+1). Expression: (–1)–(–2)=(+1).
Target Floor –1, Starting Floor +3: movement needed is –4. Expression: (–1)–(+3)=(–4).
Target Floor +2, Starting Floor –2: movement needed is +4. Expression: (+2)–(–2)=(+4).
Computing +2000–(–200): going from –200 to +2000 on the infinite lift requires pressing +2200. Reveals that subtracting a negative equals adding a positive.
UNL showing 85+(–60)=25 by marking –60 movement from +85.
UNL showing (–100)–(+250)=–350 by interpreting subtraction as missing addend.
Breaking journey from 2 to –3: (a) 2→0 is –2, (b) 0→–3 is –3; total movement is –3+(–2)=–5. Shows –3–2=–5 with no subtraction in second form.
Lay out 5 positive and 8 negative tokens; remove 5 zero pairs; 3 negative tokens remain. (+5)+(–8)=–3.
From 7 negatives, remove 5; 2 negatives remain. (–7)–(–5)=–2. Also prompts: is this the same as (–7)+(+5)?
5 positives, need to remove 6 — not enough. Add one zero pair; now remove 6 positives; 1 negative left. (+5)–(+6)=–1.
4 positives, need to remove 6 negatives — none present. Add 6 zero pairs (6 positives + 6 negatives); remove 6 negatives; 10 positives remain. +4–(–6)=+10.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.1 p.245
enumerated_exercises.description: Start from Floor +2 and press –3. Where do you reach? Write an expression.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.1 p.245
enumerated_exercises.description: Evaluate seven expressions (a–g) as Starting Floor + Movement: (+1)+(+4), (+4)+(+1), (+4)+(–3), (–1)+(+2), (–1)+(+1), 0+(+2), 0+(–2).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.1 p.245
enumerated_exercises.description: Find multiple starting floors and the movements needed to reach Floor –5; write expressions of the form (starting)+(movement)=–5.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.246
enumerated_exercises.description: Evaluate four expressions (a–d) as combined button presses: (+1)+(+4), (+4)+(+1), (+4)+(–3)+(–2), (–1)+(+2)+(–3).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.1 p.247
enumerated_exercises.description: Compare six pairs of integers using < or > by referring to the Building of Fun: –2 vs +5, –5 vs +4, –5 vs –3, +6 vs –6, 0 vs –4, 0 vs +4.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.1 p.247
enumerated_exercises.description: Compare six pairs of larger integers with < or >: –10 vs –12, +17 vs –10, 0 vs –20, +9 vs –9, –25 vs –7, +15 vs –17.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.1 p.247
enumerated_exercises.description: Given Floor A=–12, Floor D=–1, Floor E=+1 on a vertical number line, find the numbers of Floors B, C, F, G, and H.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.1 p.247
enumerated_exercises.description: Mark four floors (–7, –4, +3, –10) on a vertical building diagram.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.249
enumerated_exercises.description: Complete ten subtraction expressions (a–j) as movement from Starting Floor to Target Floor: (+1)–(+4), 0–(+2), (+4)–(+1), 0–(–2), (+4)–(–3), (–4)–(–3), (–1)–(+2), (–2)–(–2), (–1)–(+1), (+3)–(–3).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.251
enumerated_exercises.description: Complete seven expressions using the mine/larger number context (a–g): (+40)+__=+200, (+40)+__=–200, (–50)+__=+200, (–50)+__=–200, (–200)–(–40), (+200)–(+40), (–200)–(+40). Verify using the mineshaft.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.251
Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.255
enumerated_exercises.description: Use unmarked number lines to evaluate four expressions: –125+(–30), +105–(–55), +80–(–150), –99–(–200).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.1 p.253
enumerated_exercises.description: Mark 3 positive and 3 negative numbers on a given number line (–10 to +10).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.1 p.253
enumerated_exercises.description: Write down the 3 marked negative numbers in boxes.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.1 p.254
enumerated_exercises.description: Is 2 > –3? Why? Is –2 < 3? Why? Justify using the number line.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.1 p.254
enumerated_exercises.description: Compute six integer expressions: –5+0, 7+(–7), –10+20, 10–20, 7–(–7), –8–(–10).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.257
enumerated_exercises.description: Complete four additions using token diagrams: (+6)+(+4), (–3)+(–2), (+5)+(–7), (–2)+(+6).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.257
enumerated_exercises.description: Cancel zero pairs in two given token sets (a: 3 positive + 5 negative; b: 6 positive + 3 negative). State which floor the attendant is on and write the corresponding addition statement.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.258
Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.258
Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.259
enumerated_exercises.description: Subtract –3–(+5) using tokens. How many zero pairs are needed? What is the result?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.259
Figure it Out Q1, section 10.3 p.260
enumerated_exercises.description: Bank account: start at ₹0, add credits of ₹30, ₹40, ₹50 and debits of ₹40, ₹50, ₹60. What is the balance?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.3 p.260
enumerated_exercises.description: Bank account: start at ₹0, debits of ₹1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 and a single credit of ₹256. What is the balance?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.3 p.260
enumerated_exercises.description: Why is it generally better to keep a positive bank balance? When might a temporary negative balance be worthwhile?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.3 p.261
enumerated_exercises.description: Read heights A–G from a geographical cross-section chart and fill in the values.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.3 p.261
enumerated_exercises.description: Identify the highest and lowest points in the geographical cross-section.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.3 p.261
enumerated_exercises.description: Write points A–G in decreasing and increasing order of heights.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.3 p.261
enumerated_exercises.description: What is the highest point above sea level on Earth and its height?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 10.3 p.261
enumerated_exercises.description: What is the lowest point on Earth with respect to sea level? What is its (negative) height?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.3 p.262
enumerated_exercises.description: Research and find places in India where temperatures go below 0°C. What do they have in common and why?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.3 p.262
enumerated_exercises.description: Match four temperatures (14°C, 8°C, –2°C, –4°C) with four times of day in Leh, Ladakh in November.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.4 p.263
enumerated_exercises.description: Compute the four border sums (top row, bottom row, left column, right column) for the second hollow integer grid and identify the border sum.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.4 p.264
enumerated_exercises.description: Complete three hollow integer grids to achieve specified border sums: +4, –2, –4.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.4 p.264
enumerated_exercises.description: Find more than one way to fill the border-sum –4 grid.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.4 p.264
enumerated_exercises.description: Which grids can be filled in multiple ways? What could be the reason?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 10.4 p.264
enumerated_exercises.description: Make a border integer square puzzle and challenge classmates.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: In the amazing grid, try the circle-and-strike game afresh with different chosen numbers. Does the final sum change?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: Play the circle-and-strike game on two other provided 4×4 grids (arithmetic progression grids).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: What is special about these grids — is the 'magic' in the numbers, the arrangement, or both? Can you make more such grids?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: Write all integers between four given pairs in increasing order: (a) 0 and –7, (b) –4 and 4, (c) –8 and –15, (d) –30 and –23.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: Give three integers whose sum is –8.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: Two dice with faces –1, 2, –3, 4, –5, 6. Find the numbers between –10 and +12 that cannot be obtained as a sum of the two dice.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.4 p.265
enumerated_exercises.description: Solve eight integer arithmetic expressions arranged in a 2×4 table: 8–13, (–8)–(13), (–13)–(–8), (–13)+(–8), 8+(–13), (–8)–(–13), (13)–8, 13–(–8).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 10.4 p.265-266
enumerated_exercises.description: Find three historical years: (a) 150 years before the present year, (b) 2200 years before the present year (no year 0), (c) 320 years after 680 BCE.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 10.4 p.266
Figure it Out Q7, section 10.4 p.266
enumerated_exercises.description: From six integer cards (+1, +7, +18, –5, –2, –9), construct an addition/subtraction expression whose value is closest to –30.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 10.4 p.266
enumerated_exercises.description: Determine whether each of six types of integer addition/subtraction combinations [(positive)–(negative), (positive)+(negative), (negative)+(negative), (negative)–(negative), (negative)–(positive), (negative)+(positive)] is always positive, always negative, or can be either.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 10.4 p.266
enumerated_exercises.description: A string of 100 tokens in a repeating pattern (+++––). What is the total value of the string?

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.5 p.268
enumerated_exercises.description: Explain each of Brahmagupta's rules for addition and subtraction in terms of Bela's Building of Fun or the number line.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.5 p.268
enumerated_exercises.description: Give your own examples of each of Brahmagupta's rules for addition and subtraction.
